frameworks.name: motion energy model
frameworks.one_line: Directional motion-energy filtering as the front-end summary of dot motion.

frameworks.name: statistical moment representation
frameworks.one_line: Tests whether vision encodes mean, variance, skewness, kurtosis of motion distributions.

frameworks.name: ensemble / summary statistics perception
frameworks.one_line: Prior claim that higher-order moments shape static texture and surface perception.

core_question: Does the visual system represent higher-order statistical moments (skewness, kurtosis) of dynamic random-dot motion distributions, or only low-order moments?
key_assumptions: Random-dot kinematograms can independently manipulate moments of the dot-direction distribution.
Motion-energy filtering provides a sufficient front-end account of coherent motion percepts.
Discrimination behavior reflects the moments actually encoded by early motion processing.
manipulations: Mean direction of the dot-direction distribution.
Variance of individual dot displacement angles.
key_findings: Observers discriminated changes in mean direction of coherent motion.
Observers were sensitive to variance of dot displacement angles.
Observers were insensitive to skewness of the motion distribution.
Observers were insensitive to kurtosis of the motion distribution.
interpretations: Behavior is consistent with a directional motion-energy model that discards higher-order moments early in the visual hierarchy.
Use of higher-order distributional moments is not a general property of visual perception across static and dynamic stimuli.
